TextENGNew: "...Please explain, Kanou-san! This sudden order to halt the investigation—I can't accept it!"
Thanks to Chief Yamaoki's consideration, we had moved into his office, where I raised my voice and shouted into the receiver.
It was utterly unreasonable. The director's orders may be absolute, but this was not something I could simply accept and move on from.
Wasn't this mission supposed to be a covert investigation, precisely to keep it beyond the reach of those above?
Then why...?!
"Kanou-san. Finally—through the cooperation of so many people, and... sacrifices—we're on the verge of grasping something."
"The threat of 'Oyashiro-sama,' the deity worshipped in Hinamizawa village, and what the truth behind it really is—we're so close to understanding! Please, just a little longer! I'm begging you!"
'...Akasaka-kun.'
From the other end of the line, I heard Kanou-san let out a sigh.
From the weight of that exhale alone, I already sensed the decision would not be reversed—and yet I couldn't stop myself from pushing back.
"Kanou-san!"
'...This is a director's order. Do you understand what it means to defy it?'
"Yes... I think I do. But if we end the investigation here, it will have all been for nothing! Nothing will remain!"
"If at all possible, please give me a chance to petition Director Kataoka myself! That's how important this case is, Kanou-san!!"

'I believe I understand how you feel. I myself have asked Director Kataoka to reconsider, time and again. ...But today, word came. Naming both you and me directly—from the Security Bureau of the National Police Agency.'
"The National Police Agency? ...Why?!"

The Security Bureau of the NPA serves as the supreme authority over the public security police forces across the country. My own section—the Metropolitan Police Department's Public Security Bureau—falls under its jurisdiction, and local public security units operate under its directives.
But... how had they managed to track our investigative activities?
'Right now, a series of strange incidents is occurring throughout your area of Kakiuchi City, yes?'

'Some of those incidents have been designated inter-prefectural cases, with NPA personnel dispatched to handle them. It seems that in the course of that, information reached them about a detective conducting interviews with people from Hinamizawa.'
changeReason: 'wide area incidents' changed to 'inter-prefectural cases'

TextENGNew: "B-but... why would the Security Bureau be ordering us to stop because of that?"

"Our investigation at this point is aimed at uncovering the existence of those involved with the village residents who were behind the Hinamizawa Catastrophe! The Security Bureau should have no grounds to interfere!"
changeReason: 'Great Hinamizawa Disaster' changed to 'Hinamizawa Catastrophe'

TextENGNew: 'According to the NPA, an information blackout is being imposed on this case due to its social impact as an inter-prefectural incident. The official reason they gave is that our activities risk interfering with their investigation...'
"The 'official reason'...? ...Then what's the real reason?"

'......
Akasaka-kun, is anyone with you?'
"No... I'm alone."
Ooishi-san and Chief Yamaoki were waiting in the next room—considerate enough to give me space, given the Public Security involvement.
...And when Kanou-san felt the need for such a preamble, it meant what he was about to share was classified information he had uncovered on his own.
Sensing the gravity of it, tension shot through my entire body—but I steeled myself and told him, '...Please. Go on.'
'...The one who applied pressure was the Cabinet Intelligence and Research Office. We don't have the name of the specific officer yet, but there's no doubt about it.'
changeReason: 'Cabinet Research Division' changed to 'Cabinet Intelligence and Research Office'

TextENGNew: The Cabinet Intelligence and Research Office...?!
changeReason: 'Cabinet Research Division' changed to 'Cabinet Intelligence and Research Office'

TextENGNew: An intelligence agency tasked with gathering and analyzing information to support the Prime Minister and cabinet members on matters of critical policy—what could it possibly want with the activities of a public security investigation?!

Moreover, the subjects of our investigation were, with all due respect, a remote rural village and a suburban town. They held no particular economic importance to the nation, nor were they subject to any special governmental designation.
Then why...?! If anything, this is even more inexplicable than the Security Bureau's stop order!!
"Kanou-san. ...Can you tell me why?"

'...I'm sorry. Going any deeper poses too great a risk, even for me. All I can say with certainty is that someone connected to that organization has been operating behind the scenes and applying pressure to the NPA and to us.'
"...I see."
'I understand your feelings, and it pains me. I regret that I drew you into work that asked too much of you.'
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: '...But I want you to understand.'

'If CIRO is involved, that means this matter has become entangled in political judgments at the level of the state itself. ...You know firsthand from the previous case what that kind of influence means, don't you?'
"...Yes. But—wait a moment, Kanou-san. What did you just say?"
'Hm? That this case, like the previous one, has political forces at work—'
"Not that—what you said before! You just said 'CIRO'...!"
'Ah, yes—it's the abbreviation for the Cabinet Intelligence and Research Office. The English name is "Cabinet Intelligence and Research Office"—CIRO for short.'
'You know it, don't you?'
...I know it. It's an abbreviation commonly used in our line of work.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: The only reason I hadn't made the connection was that it had seemed entirely unrelated to this particular investigation.
But—
'Those were the words Inspector Minai whispered to the paramedics. "Shiro" and "Naichō"—'
changeReason: Changed from translated 'CIRO'/'cabinet office' to romanized 'Shiro'/'Naicho'; rank changed from 'Chief Inspector' to 'Inspector'.

TextENGNew: '—?! What's the matter, Akasaka-kun?! Hello? Hello!—'
With a force that could shatter bones, I slammed my fist against the chief's desk.
The one who had forced me to halt my investigation...
changeReason: Changed from plural 'They were the ones' to singular 'The one who'. Minor grammatical difference.

TextENGNew: The one who had imposed an information blackout on the Hinamizawa-related incidents, treating them as inter-prefectural cases...
changeReason: 'wide area incidents' changed to 'inter-prefectural cases'

TextENGNew: And... and!! The one who had taken Inspector Minai's life...!!
changeReason: Title changed from 'Chief Inspector' to 'Inspector'

TextENGNew: All of them... all of them!!!
What was the state hiding beneath the Hinamizawa Catastrophe?!
changeReason: 'Great Hinamizawa Disaster' changed to 'Hinamizawa Catastrophe'

TextENGNew: Two thousand villagers killed, and even those who escaped were being dragged into bizarre incidents, their lives taken one by one!!
And just to keep the secret hidden—murdering the detective who had come close to uncovering it—what in God's name were they scheming?!
And then... and then! Even with this much understanding of their trail, was I supposed to slink away from here empty-handed like a beaten dog?!
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: Then what had everything I'd done up until now been for?!!

"...Damn it all,
Damn it all!!!"
Was this kind of injustice... just allowed to carry on?!
This... outrage... why?!!
Saturday, July 9, Showa 58
changeReason: Date format changed from Western year to Showa era

TextENGNew: ...Last evening, a torrential rain had poured down starting in the late afternoon.
Perhaps because of that, today—despite the rain having stopped—the air was somewhat cool for July, and my throat had felt raw since a while ago.
(...I might be coming down with a cold.)
Come to think of it, when I'd gotten out of my hotel bed, my body had felt slightly heavy. ...Though perhaps that was just my own dejection making it seem so.
"...Pathetic."
I sat down on one of the benches in the break room, turning a lukewarm can of tea over in both hands, muttering to myself with a bitter laugh.
'—Cheer up, please.'
Suddenly.
The image of Inspector Minai sitting in the chair across from me, smiling—flickered into view... and vanished just as quickly.
changeReason: Title changed from 'Chief Inspector' to 'Inspector'

TextENGNew: Come to think of it, she had sat in that very seat once before, eating a mountain of hamburgers.
I had been meaning to warn her someday that a diet like that would absolutely land her in the hospital with lifestyle disease before long. But that chance never came.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: She had gone somewhere beyond the reach of such concerns...
"There's no way I can just cheer up. Not that easily..."
Answering the woman who was no longer there, the helplessness and the injustice of it welled up until I couldn't bear it—and before I knew it, I was crushing the can in my grip.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: ...Afterward, I had told Ooishi-san and the others that the order to halt the investigation had come down from Public Security.
Ooishi-san was understandably disappointed, but true to his veteran nature, he recovered quickly—and in fact tried to lift my spirits, speaking in a deliberately cheerful tone.
'...It's a shame, but if it's an order from above, there's nothing to be done. I'll keep investigating on my own, and I'll let you know the moment anything comes up.'
'Ooishi-san... please be careful. I could not bear to hear that you, too, had been cut down.'
'N'hhuhhuhhu! Thank you for your concern. ...Yes, I won't die.'
'I can't afford to die so easily... because the life I carry isn't mine alone.'
Saying so, Ooishi-san struck his own chest with his great fist—a solid thud.
Contemplating the deep grief carved into that gesture, I found I had nothing to say.
'Inspector Akasaka. We will continue to offer every cooperation we can on our end, so please leave the rest to us. We will arrest the culprit without fail and bring him to you.'
"Chief Yamaoki..."
'Madoka-kun too... leave her to us.'
Both of them carried a grief and anger even greater than my own—to be encouraged by them was almost too much to bear. All I could manage was: '...I'm counting on you.'
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: However... I told neither of them what Inspector Minai's final words had referred to. Not a single word of it.
changeReason: Title changed from 'Chief Inspector' to 'Inspector'

TextENGNew: ...I could not bring myself to.
How could I possibly tell them that the justice we had believed in, the nation we had been proud to serve, was capable of condoning the taking of lives for political purposes?
In the course of fulfilling my duties in Public Security, I had often felt the weight of my own powerlessness—the injustice of the world.
But when it came down to it... what, exactly, was the police here to do?
To serve the public?
To serve the state?
Or to serve a handful of the powerful?

I had long since been disabused of the naive notion that there was any such thing as absolute justice. But the conviction that I worked for those who believed in justice, for ordinary people who simply wanted to live in peace—that, I thought, had never wavered.

And no matter how many obstacles stood in my way, no matter how many detours I was forced to take, I had always believed that in the end, the righteous would prevail—and I had pushed myself forward in the hope of helping them do so.
And yet...
Was it to taste this kind of bitter, maddening helplessness that I had become a detective...?
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: I drained the last of the can and lobbed it toward the wastebasket.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: But the arc was slightly off, and it caught the rim and tumbled to the floor, rolling away with a grating, unpleasant sound.
And when it came to rest, I looked down at where it had stopped—and glanced up to find:
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: "...Ooishi-san."
Without my noticing, Ooishi-san had appeared in the doorway.
"You're in quite a state, aren't you, Akasaka-san. On days like this, beer's a better fit than tea, don't you think? N'hhuhhuhhu..."
Saying that, Ooishi-san picked up the can and tossed it in the wastebasket, then settled into the seat across from me and drew some documents from his briefcase.

"I hope you won't find this unpleasant to hear as a sort of parting gift. ...Yesterday, I managed to get hold of the school record cards that Kimiyoshi Natsumi had while she attended junior high and high school here in Okinomiya."
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: "I thought you might want to take a look as well."
"...You managed to get your hands on those?"
"N'hhuhhuhhu! Well, the high school handed theirs over right away once I showed a warrant, but the junior high—oh, that was a struggle."
"Fortunately, her homeroom teacher happened to be an old acquaintance of mine, so I was able to get their cooperation somehow."
The search warrant had apparently been issued specially through the neighboring prefecture's police, thanks to Chief Yamaoki's efforts.
The documents Ooishi-san spread open contained grades and evaluations for Kimiyoshi Natsumi.
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: "So, looking at the high school records, well—they're fairly standard evaluations you'd expect for a female student. Nothing particularly remarkable."
"Quiet, diligent, a good listener. Decent social relationships, grades somewhere in the upper-middle to lower-upper range. ...An ordinary girl you'd find anywhere."
"...That's true."
I cast my mind back to the impression she had made when I met her briefly once before.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: She had seemed like a girl who liked reading—quiet, reserved in manner.
She appeared to be a bit shy, and the way she spoke carried a certain lack of confidence—but that was nothing unusual for girls her age, and nothing worth particular note.
—However.
"But look here—in her second year of junior high, things are quite different—not just a little, but considerably. Here, take a look at this."
"...Is this really Kimiyoshi Natsumi?"
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: I stared at the photograph affixed to the page, genuinely taken aback.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: The Kimiyoshi Natsumi in the photo wore her hair long and loose, with bangs grown out far enough to cover her eyes—she looked like a ghost girl.
changeReason: Name order changed from Western to Japanese (Natsumi Kimiyoshi -> Kimiyoshi Natsumi).

TextENGNew: "Yes. And do you know what her nickname was, from first through second year of junior high? They called her 'Zashiki-warashi.'"
changeReason: Cultural explanation 'poltergeist' removed from Zashiki-warashi

TextENGNew: "Zashiki-warashi..."
"Probably on account of the gloomy image she gave off."
changeReason: Minor rewording of Ooishi's comment about gloomy image.

TextENGNew: "For one thing, she was completely antisocial—she was almost always alone."
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: "When someone spoke to her, her responses were sluggish, and whenever something displeased her, she would stop talking altogether. She frequently missed school as well, and the homeroom teacher made several home visits..."

At that point, it went beyond mere passivity—it meant she was a genuinely problematic student. A clearly isolated presence in the class poisons the atmosphere and can easily become the trigger for bullying.
"And apparently, when the first-year homeroom teacher asked why she didn't play with the other students, she gave this answer: 'Because Mother gets angry.'"
"...Her mother would get angry?!"
"Of course, when the teacher consulted her mother—Kimiyoshi Haruko—on the matter, afterward Natsumi never said anything more to suggest problems with her mother."
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: "...But her behavior never changed at all. The mother had probably given her a strict scolding: 'Don't say bad things about your parents.'"
"...Proof of that is that she apparently started withdrawing more and more, refusing to even speak with her teachers..."
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: "What a terrible thing..."
It was a textbook example of a household where discipline had gone too far.
Natsumi had no intention of criticizing her mother, I'm sure of that.
She had been asked a question and answered it honestly. And if the mother had taken that as a warning and made some effort to change, the problem might have been resolved surprisingly simply.
But what if the mother hadn't taken it as a warning from her daughter—but instead felt that the child had embarrassed her by tattling, and scolded her for it?
(That child would surely stop trusting anyone at all.)
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: Still too young emotionally, she would have come to think: "If I tell the teacher, I'll be scolded again."
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: And she would stop speaking to anyone at all. After all, no matter who she talked to, she could never know when or why her mother might get angry.
And so she would drive herself further and further into isolation...
"But then... why did Kimiyoshi Natsumi's personality change so suddenly? Was there some kind of trigger?"
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: "Hmm, well... the junior high teacher said they didn't really know either. So I asked when roughly her personality had changed so noticeably, and the answer was: it was after she was hospitalized three years ago."
"Hospitalized...? What was she admitted for?"
"Anemia treatment, they said. Apparently Kimiyoshi Natsumi had a pattern of fainting suddenly without warning, so she was hospitalized for nearly two months to treat it."
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: "Fortunately it coincided with summer vacation, so it had little impact on her schoolwork."
Anemia...

A condition where insufficient iron in the blood—whether congenital or acquired—leads to oxygen deficiency, causing sudden loss of consciousness during strenuous activity or regular spells of light-headedness.
It isn't incurable, but because it can develop concurrently with other conditions, diagnosis and treatment can sometimes be a lengthy process.

"Still... could improving physically through hospitalization really lead to such a sudden improvement in her mental state as well? Even as a side effect of poor health, something about that feels off."
"Well, the teacher's assumption at the time was that the hospitalization had given the mother a chance to reflect and soften her strict approach to parenting. So they didn't probe too deeply."

"...And she successfully enrolled in the local high school. The teacher mentioned that there had once been talk of sending her to a prestigious preparatory school in the city, but the hospitalization apparently put an end to that."
"...Was sending her to a city preparatory school the mother's idea?"

"Yes. She was apparently quite an ambitious education-focused mother, and she reportedly butted heads fairly often with Natsumi's grandmother—Kimiyoshi Aki—who prioritized Natsumi's health above all else. It was said to be common gossip around the neighborhood."
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: "In fact, the high school teacher said that besides the grandmother's fear of 'Oyashiro-sama's curse' being part of the reason for the move to Kakiuchi City, her strong opposition on grounds of Natsumi's health was another major factor."
Life in the city runs at a different speed and rhythm than life in a small town.

It would only be natural for Aki to worry that Natsumi—who had finally overcome her isolation and was able to interact normally again—might revert to her old self if thrust into an unfamiliar environment.
...Her grandmother's concern was entirely understandable.
But the mother, Haruko, had taken her daughter's improvement in personality as an opportunity to push her even further into a 'better' environment.
Certainly, if one intended to find work in the countryside and settle down there, academic credentials wouldn't matter much.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: But if the plan was to go to the city and work for a company with a reputable name, one would need to graduate from an appropriately prestigious university.
And for that, attending a competitive preparatory school in the city would give a significant advantage on university entrance exams...
(I myself had not been entirely immune to that particular pressure.)
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: ...From the earlier records I'd reviewed, Natsumi's mother—Kimiyoshi Haruko—had graduated from a local university that wasn't particularly highly ranked. Her husband, Fuyu, was from the same university.
changeReason: Name reading changed: 'Touji' to 'Fuyu'; Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: Most likely, the mother's ambitions were rooted in a practical desire to spare her daughter the hardships she and her husband had faced in the job market because of their academic backgrounds.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: I didn't think that was wrong. ...But whether Kimiyoshi Natsumi herself had accepted and gone along with it willingly, or was simply enduring it out of fear of her mother's wrath—those two possibilities carried entirely different meanings.
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: If that frustration had gradually transformed into hatred, resentment—if it had accumulated layer upon layer of repressed emotion, and then been unleashed or detonated by some triggering event—what then?
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: The active emotion that such a rupture would generate: 'murderous intent.'
...Or, as a passive counterpart: 'rejection'—
"? What is it, Akasaka-san?"
"Ooishi-san... this is purely hypothetical, but—"

Keeping in mind the analysis results of Kimiyoshi Natsumi's regular medication—the results Inspector Minai had asked Matsugaoka of the Gifu Prefectural Police to obtain—I outlined a theory to Ooishi-san.
changeReason: Title changed from 'Chief Inspector' to 'Inspector'; Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: "The medication Kimiyoshi Natsumi had been taking regularly was anti-excitatory, anti-neural-transmission suppressants—that was the substance identified, yes?"
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: "...If so, then might it not be precisely because she began taking that medication that her personality changed so dramatically?"
"H...? What do you mean by that..."
"What I mean is this: perhaps her condition wasn't anemia at all—perhaps the 'fainting' she experienced was syncope."

It's a commonly confused distinction, but fainting due to psychological causes is not the same as anemia. It is a transient loss of consciousness—a phenomenon where the mind, so to speak, 'trips a circuit breaker.'

For example: when a violent impulse—a destructive urge, a murderous thought, a drive toward self-harm—causes a 'panicking instinct' to reach for the wrong choice, the 'rational mind' moves to suppress it.
And as one grows, the rational mind becomes strong enough to control those instincts, which is why most adults don't act impulsively or recklessly.
But what about someone in early adolescence—when that rational faculty hasn't yet fully matured?
Young people unaccustomed to the fierce surges of instinct within them can sometimes be overwhelmed by that explosive energy—and at times, the thought becomes the action.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: The most extreme manifestations of this: destructive behavior, murder, suicide...
The fact that juvenile violence and assault with no clear motive is so prevalent is likely in part because the 'instincts' are simply not yet under control.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: ...And yet, occasionally there are children whose rational faculty has developed exceptionally—whether through nature or nurture. This tendency appears more frequently in women, whose emotional maturation is said to precede that of men.
So what choices do they make to avoid that explosion of instinct?
Exactly. The emergency escape these children instinctively reach for is a 'forced shutdown of consciousness'—what we commonly call 'fainting.'
"Most likely, Kimiyoshi Natsumi had a remarkably strong capacity for endurance compared to others her age."
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: "...So when she was seized by violent impulses she might have acted on in any normal scenario, she would either withdraw inward—or if even that was too difficult, she would short-circuit her own consciousness to prevent herself from acting."
"That was what led to her self-imposed isolation during her junior high years..."
In other words, Natsumi hadn't been isolating herself because she was bad at socializing.
...She was building walls to protect others—and herself—from the explosive, impulsive emotions she might otherwise direct at the people around her.
All while suppressing, deep inside herself, her anger at the unreasonable world around her. Her grief. ...And her hatred.
"But then—what does any of this have to do with that medication Tomoe-san had asked to have analyzed?"
changeReason: Honorific changed: 'tomoe-chan' to 'tomoe-san'

TextENGNew: "Kimiyoshi Natsumi, by taking that anti-excitatory medication, was able to dissolve the dark emotions she had been storing up—the hatred directed at her mother and everyone around her."
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: "It wasn't a fundamental solution by any means. But given that her mental state did stabilize, the medication clearly had some effect."
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: "As a result, with her negative emotions dampened, her actions and thinking became steadier, and she was released from the constant strain of suppressing explosive feelings—allowing her natural brightness to reemerge..."
But the 'dark emotions' had not vanished entirely. ...Because they too were part of who Kimiyoshi Natsumi genuinely was.
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: In an ordinary adolescent, those violent and unstable 'dark emotions' compete and jostle with the composed, rational 'white emotions'—but over time, they negotiate, learn limits, and gradually merge.
But when, as in her case, two sets of emotions had been forcibly separated by medication—the 'dark emotions,' with no interference from the 'white,' would have had no chance to learn restraint.
And so, if something triggered those 'dark emotions' to wake and surface, their momentum—accumulated and untempered—might exceed what reason alone could contain...
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: "Hmm... well, forgive me for saying so, Akasaka-san, but that's quite a dramatic leap. Like something out of a mystery novel."
"But as one hypothesis—how does it hold up?"
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: "...... The logic is sound. If we could interview staff from the hospital where Kimiyoshi Natsumi was admitted at the time, we might find a breakthrough there."
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: "Whether she was hospitalized for anemia—or for treatment of a psychiatric condition. Even establishing that much could change things considerably."
changeReason: Minor rewording of Akasaka's question about hospitalization.

TextENGNew: "Do you know which hospital she was admitted to?"
"Not yet. But given the circumstances, it's certainly worth investigating."
"...Well! You've been a great help, Akasaka-san! Why not stay on and form a private detective agency together? N'hhuhhuhhu!"
"...That does sound like an option. I'll give it some thought."
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: "Hm? ...N'hahahahah! I was joking. If I went and turned such a talented member of the Metropolitan Police into a penniless private eye, the bigwigs in central personnel would have my head!"
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: "...Ooishi-san."
"Well then, I'll go track down that lead right away. I'll be in touch when I have something. Farewell."
Saying that, Ooishi-san left the break room.
I watched him go with vague, unfocused eyes, then let out a sigh and tilted my head back to stare at the ceiling.
For a while, I sat there thinking nothing in particular, my mind drifting.
A private detective agency with Ooishi-san, huh...
I had said it as a casual response, but I found I meant it.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: Being tossed around by incomprehensible orders and arbitrary restrictions from central command—if I could at least wield justice on a smaller scale, with complete freedom to act, a detective agency might be a far more satisfying life.
In that case, I could dig into the mystery of Hinamizawa more deeply, making it the top priority.
In fact, wouldn't that be the most fitting memorial I could offer to Rika-chan and Inspector Minai, who were now gone...?
changeReason: Title changed from 'Chief Inspector' to 'Inspector'

TextENGNew: —Just then.

'All stations, notification from Kakiuchi Police Headquarters to the detectives at Kakiuchi Police Station. Repeat: notification from Kakiuchi Police Headquarters to the detectives at Kakiuchi Police Station.'
changeReason: Institution changed: 'prefectural police HQ' to 'Kakiuchi Police HQ'

TextENGNew: '—Assault incident reported. Victim is a female student attending a city high school. Scene of the incident: Saeki General Hospital. All officers are to proceed immediately to emergency positions under the direction of their supervisors. Repeat—'
Saeki General Hospital—that was the place I had visited just recently regarding the medication.
And the victim was... a high school girl?!
(Could it be—!)
For an instant, Kimiyoshi Natsumi's face flashed through my mind. And then—
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: "—Ah, Inspector Akasaka! I've been looking all over for you!"
It was Detective Fujita, rushing in. He looked terribly flustered, and was breathing hard and sweating—clearly he had been running through the station looking for me.
"What is it, Detective Fujita?"
"I'll explain in the car! Please come with me, immediately!"
...Riding in Detective Fujita's car, I was taken to the scene—Saeki General Hospital.
Apparently Ooishi-san had left the station just moments earlier, so Fujita had sent a page; we were waiting for a reply.
"...Where's the suspect now?"
changeReason: 'culprit' changed to 'suspect'

TextENGNew: "Fled, it appears. It being broad daylight, there should have been people around—but apparently no one matching the description has been seen yet."
"The victim is Saeki Chisato. ...The daughter of Saeki General Hospital's director."
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: ...The outline of the incident, as Detective Fujita described it, was this.

Shortly after 2 p.m., the victim—Saeki Chisato—had arrived at the hospital with driver Yamashina. She was there to meet her father for an outing, and had been waiting in the entrance lobby until the appointed time.
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: A few minutes later, apparently recalling something, the victim told driver Yamashina 'I'll be right back' and stepped outside the hospital alone.
When the appointed time came and the father arrived, but the victim had not returned, driver Yamashina grew suspicious and began searching the area.
He found her collapsed among the shrubbery in front of the Saeki Care and Welfare Center on the hospital grounds, bleeding from the head.
She was immediately brought inside the hospital, and 119 was called at the same time.
"No weapon was found at the scene. Nothing was stolen from her either, so it doesn't appear to be a robbery."

"The victim's injuries are relatively minor—after five stitches to the head, she regained full consciousness. A preliminary interview was conducted, but was cut short midway due to her poor condition."
"...The victim didn't see the attacker's face?"

"No. Apparently she was attacked from behind without warning... she didn't have time to look. Additional questioning was attempted regarding any persons she might suspect, but it was suspended partway through due to her reported physical distress."
"I see..."
It could be a random attack, or an assault motivated by some personal grudge. ...But if so, why had they come for me?

"...The victim, Saeki Chisato, was apparently close with the Kimiyoshi Natsumi we've been looking at. Moreover, the Saeki Care and Welfare Center, where the attack took place, was where Kimiyoshi Natsumi had been working part-time until recently."
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: Two officers were stationed in front of the hospital room where the victim, Saeki Chisato, had been admitted. One of them was Detective Hanada.
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: "Ah—thank you for coming, Inspector Akasaka."
"Thank you. ...What's the situation?"
"She appeared to have been sleeping until a little while ago, but a classmate came in some time back, and they've been talking in there."
True enough—straining my ears, I could hear the sound of muffled sobbing and a calmer voice coming from within the room.
Most likely, a friend who had rushed over in concern was crying with relief, while the victim was trying to comfort her.
"...Is she up to speaking with us?"
"We have the permission of both the attending physician and the father, but... her emotional state is still not fully stable. Please try not to upset her, and keep it brief."
"Understood."
I nodded to Detective Hanada and knocked on the hospital room door. After a moment, a guarded voice from within replied, '...Who is it?'
"...I'm Detective Akasaka. Would you have a few minutes?"
Through the door came an unmistakably wary sound of breathing. A prolonged silence followed—I was beginning to worry I might be turned away—when the voice finally returned: '...Come in.'
"...Excuse me."
Inside the room, a girl lay in the bed while another in her school uniform sat beside her on a round stool. I quietly drew my police ID from my breast pocket and showed it.
"I'm terribly sorry to disturb you when you're in such poor health. I have a few questions I'd like to ask—might I have your cooperation?"
"...I'll cooperate, but—"

The victim—Saeki Chisato-san—sat up in the bed and pulled a hospital gown around her, still pressing her head with a pained expression. She exchanged a reassuring 'I'm all right' with the worried friend beside her, then turned to face me.
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: "I'm not exactly in great shape, so please don't make this too long. ...And I've already answered most things for the other detective."
"Yes, I understand that. ...May I come a little closer?"
I gave a slight bow to acknowledge her words, then moved to stand at the bedside opposite from where the friend was seated.
The friend was watching me with unconcealed hostility, something close to repulsion in her expression—whereas Chisato-san, in contrast, met my gaze with a look of quiet sadness.
"I won't waste your time. I want to ask you about a close friend of yours."
As if to say she had expected as much, Chisato-san lowered her eyes and bit her lip. I hesitated briefly over whether to name the person, conscious of the friend sitting across from us—
"...What is it you want to know about Natsumi?"
In a slightly trembling voice, it was the friend—not the silent Chisato-san—who answered.
"Forgive me, but... who are you?"
"I'm... Makimura Tamako. Like Chisato-san here, I'm Kimiyoshi Natsumi's classmate."
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: "I see. Then you as well—"
"Do you suspect Natsumi? If that's what this is, you've got the wrong person. She's not the kind of girl who could do something like this!"
"...Tamako."
"No, let me say it, Chisato! I heard it from Akira-chan! It was after those strange people called her out to that family restaurant that Natsumi started acting strange!"
"She was terrified, and she was crying!! Could those people have been your colleagues?!"
changeReason: Minor rewording of Tamako's exclamation about Natsumi's fear.

TextENGNew: Saying so, the girl across from me—Makimura Tamako-san—glared at me with open, concentrated hatred. The memory of that encounter stirred within me, and I found I could not bear it without a pang of guilt.
"It's because of you that she changed! She used to be so cheerful, so kind, so wonderful—and you ruined all of that!"
"What did she ever do to you?!"
"Tamako..."
"And while all of that was happening, I... I didn't know anything, and I... I suspected her... I said terrible things..."
"I was the one who called Chisato inconsiderate, but I was the one who understood Natsumi the least of all of us...!!"
"...That's not true—"
"...I want to apologize! I want to tell her I'm sorry I couldn't do anything, sorry I misunderstood her and pulled away, I want to say I'm sorry to Natsumi!! Otherwise I... I can't...!!"
In great, heaving sobs.
Tears streaming down her face, Tamako-san collapsed forward and wept, completely undone.
The depth of her grief and the tenderness of her love for her friend—I found I had no words to offer against them.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: "...As you have said, it was we who questioned Kimiyoshi Natsumi-san. I also acknowledge that the way we went about it was excessive. ...Words alone can't undo what was done, but please—allow me to apologize."
"...An apology... it's too late for an apology...!!"
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: "Stop, Tamako... The detective had his reasons. It's not fair to blame him, and honestly—"
Chisato-san tried to comfort the weeping Tamako-san, while seeming to recall something else that pained her—her own expression clouded with distress. Watching her, I pressed a little further:
"...How much do you know about Kimiyoshi Natsumi-san's situation?"
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: "I understand you don't want to speak of it. I've been involved with this case long enough to have witnessed many tragedies, and I understand that all too well."
"...And I've asked myself, more than once, whether my involvement has simply brought more suffering to those around me—whether I should have stopped midway."
"But please, listen to me, Saeki-san! Your friend—Natsumi-san—is still alive."
"The dead cannot return. But those who still live can yet be saved! ...Am I wrong?"
"What I want is not to arrest her. What I want is to save her. That much—please believe me."
With a small flinch, Chisato-san's shoulders tensed, and her grip on the blanket tightened.
That's right...
As I was speaking, I found myself remembering her words.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: '...If possible, I want to save her. I can't help but feel like she and I aren't so different...'
Inspector Minai had cared about Kimiyoshi Natsumi to the very end.
changeReason: Title changed from 'Chief Inspector' to 'Inspector'; Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: Through my own inadequacy, this investigation would likely come to nothing. ...Even so—if nothing else, I wanted to do my very best for the young woman that Inspector Minai had wanted to save!
That was the least I could do... the only small way I had of easing her regret...

"...Natsumi-san is in pain right now. And yet she is fighting desperately against the nightmare, trying to hold back from committing more violence! The fact that she struck you and fled—that's the proof! Isn't that right?"
"If the nightmare had truly taken full control, she would have kept attacking until she killed you! She didn't go that far. ...And that's why I still believe there's a way to save her!!"
"Detective... sir...!"
Gasping, Chisato-san's eyes went wide. Tamako-san likewise averted her gaze and pressed her fist tightly against her chest.
...Yes.
Even as I was talking, from the moment I walked into this room, I had been all but certain that the person who had attacked Saeki Chisato-san was Kimiyoshi Natsumi.
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: And having listened to the two friends speak, I was now fully convinced.
They knew. ...They knew who the culprit was.
And yet they were hiding it. Going further—trying to shield her.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: That might well be a friendship filled with extraordinary compassion. But—!

"...Saeki-san. Natsumi-san will certainly be arrested for what happened today. And to rebuild any semblance of her old life, she would have to endure a suffering that would cost her every drop of blood she has..."
No... to speak honestly, anything like her old life was almost certainly out of reach.
It would be suffering severe enough to make ending one's life seem the easier path. That is what would stand before her.
"...But if she commits further violence, there will be no turning back this time! And the only one who can stop that—is you! There is no one else!!"
...Desperately.
Forcing down the surge of emotion in my words, steadying my breathing, I leaned against the bed and looked into her face.
...Perhaps what I was trying to do here was nothing more than futile resistance.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: But even so—everything wasn't lost. It couldn't be.
If it were, then everything Inspector Minai had felt and carried for this girl would be too unbearable to contemplate...
changeReason: Title changed from 'Chief Inspector' to 'Inspector'

TextENGNew: That's why! We had to stop any further violence—no matter what...!!
And then,
"...One more time. Just once more. The person who attacked you—did you see their face...?"
Again and again she seemed to waver, hesitate—Chisato-san inhaled in labored gasps, and from her lips came a low, trembling murmur.
It may not have been a very long silence.
But for me, and for the two friends of Kimiyoshi Natsumi standing before me, it felt as though time had stopped entirely—as though the air itself had frozen and locked us in place.
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: The only sound that reached my ears was the faint evening cry of the higurashi...
"...The one who... hit me from behind—"
And then.
Clutching the hands Tamako-san had placed over hers, Chisato-san said it in a small, hushed voice—almost a whisper.
"......Natsu—mi. It was Natsumi. Natsumi came from behind me and... struck me—!"

'Attention all Kakiuchi units! Emergency mobilization! All investigators are to proceed immediately to search for and apprehend suspect Kimiyoshi Natsumi in connection with the assault at Saeki General Hospital! Repeat!—'
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: The police sirens blared, red lights flashing.
'...Dispatch investigators to the Kimiyoshi residence! In the event Kimiyoshi Natsumi is located, strongly encourage voluntary surrender and accompanying us willingly!'
changeReason: Name order changed to Japanese order

TextENGNew: 'Given that the suspect is a minor, all investigators are to avoid arrest as much as possible even in the event of resistance! Take note of this directive!'
"Fujita car here. Order received—heading to the scene immediately!"
With Detective Fujita driving, I sat in the passenger seat as the scenery outside blurred and streamed backward.
Whether we would make it in time or not—that was already in the hands of fate.
And yet, this moment had been reached through the sweat, the tears, and the blood of so many.
I wanted it to end here.
I truly did. Here—at last.
"...Inspector Akasaka."
"Yes?"
Amid the wail of the sirens, setting down the radio receiver, Detective Fujita called out to me in a tone utterly out of keeping with the noise and urgency around us—quiet, almost gentle.
And then,
"I know this is a strange thing to say right now. ...But, Akasaka-san—please rise through the ranks."
"It was something Minai-san always said. 'With only middling authority, you can't do the right thing even when you know what's right. That's why I have to climb higher too.'"
"She was going to make herself recognized as capable and advance... for exactly that reason."
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: "...Fujita-san."

"I'm counting on you, Akasaka-san. Because Minai-san was always talking about you. That she wanted to work hard and hone her skills until she could be a worthy subordinate to someone as capable as you."
changeReason: Old says Minai 'wanted to work under' Akasaka; new says 'be a worthy subordinate.' Similar sentiment.

TextENGNew: "...Please don't forget her feelings."
"...... Of course I won't. I will never, ever forget...!"
The rising heat within me stirred something fierce and resolute, and I fixed my gaze hard on the road rushing straight ahead.
changeReason: Translation substantially rewritten with very different wording

TextENGNew: ...That's right.
Like Ooishi-san... I couldn't run from this either.
Yukiye. Rika-chan. Inspector Minai.
changeReason: Title changed from 'Chief Inspector' to 'Inspector'

TextENGNew: I carry the lives and dreams of so many people.
And... Miyuki.
When I had agonized over the loneliness I was causing her, Inspector Minai had encouraged me—someday, she'll understand.
changeReason: Title changed from 'Chief Inspector' to 'Inspector'

TextENGNew: Yes... someday, I want her to understand.
That I threw myself into this work as a detective with conviction, with purpose—
"I will rise. Absolutely. ...I'll give everything I have, until it kills me—!!"
At those words, Detective Fujita's sharp face broke into a smile, and he gave a nod.
At that moment—
'Fujita car, please respond! Fujita-san, Akasaka-san! Either of you, please respond!!!'
Suddenly, Madoka-san's voice burst through the radio.

...From the indicator light, it was a direct channel—not broadcast to other vehicles. Combined with the urgency in her voice, it warned us that whatever she was bringing was serious. We exchanged a glance.
"This is Fujita car. It's me—Akasaka. What happened?"
'Just now, a call came from Matsugaoka-san at the Gifu Prefectural Police forensics division! At noon today, a severed woman's head was found on a mountain road in Gifu Prefecture!'
changeReason: Character name changed from 'Matsuoka' to 'Matsugaoka'

TextENGNew: 'K-Kaoru-san received the news and told me to pass it on to you! And there—um—'
"Calm down. What was at the scene?"

'A-among the objects left at the scene, they found a hitogata—a paper doll—with the characters for "Kimiyoshi" written on it! The age hasn't been officially released yet, but reports say an elderly woman, around seventy!'
"Ah, Akasaka-san! Does that mean—?!"
There was no need for Detective Fujita to press me. ...The same thing had already come to mind for me.
'Ah! There's a breaking news bulletin on TV right now! I contacted Ooishi-san too, but I can't reach him! As soon as contact is made, I'll have him head your way immediately!!'
"Understood! We'll push forward to the scene!"
"Roger that!!"
We still had time—we had to make time! We would make it in time!
That conviction gripping my heart, I clenched both fists tight.
